refined_fr.refined_fr: Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.
refined_fr.rationale: The candidate FR bundles three distinct obligations: (1) terrain analysis, (2) obstacle identification, and (3) work-zone representation. Each is a separate functional concern with different data sources, processing logic, and stakeholder consumption patterns. The refined FR is solution-neutral (avoids 'mapping/modeling' terminology which implies a specific DP) and focuses on the core problem: making spatial features available in a structured, queryable form. Splitting into atomic sub-FRs allows independent verification and design of each concern.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Confirm that a worksite model exists in a defined schema/format, contains spatial coordinates and feature classifications, and is accessible to downstream planning and execution functions per acceptance criteria.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain elevation and surface properties across the worksite.
FR-NGHE-006.2: Identify and locate static obstacles (structures, vegetation, utilities) within the worksite boundary.
FR-NGHE-006.3: Define and represent active work zones and their operational constraints.
FR-NGHE-006.4: Persist and retrieve worksite spatial data in a format agreed with planning and execution stakeholders.
refined_fr.note: The refined FR is atomic at the system level; the sub-FRs are atomic functional concerns that together satisfy the acceptance criterion. Each sub-FR can be independently designed, verified, and traced to DPs and IMs.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data through multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; store as a structured spatial graph with feature attributes (geometry, classification, safety constraints); expose via query interface for downstream planning/execution consumers.

DP2: Decompose worksite into discrete zones; assign zone-level feature inventories (obstacles, hazards, access points) via manual annotation or semi-automated detection; represent as hierarchical zone model with feature registries; provide zone-based query API.

DP3: Capture worksite features as a dense point cloud with semantic labels; apply voxelization and octree indexing for efficient spatial queries; maintain feature metadata (safety class, operational constraint) in parallel lookup tables; expose via spatial query service.

DP4: Establish a real-time feature observation pipeline: continuous sensing → feature detection → incremental model update → versioned snapshots; maintain both current state and historical feature evolution; provide time-indexed query interface for planning horizon alignment.

DP5: Integrate worksite safety plan and operational policies as explicit constraints embedded in the spatial model; represent as constraint layers (no-go zones, speed-limited regions, equipment-restricted areas) overlaid on feature geometry; expose constraint-aware query interface that filters features by policy applicability.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1 + DP5 (integrated): Acquire spatial feature data through multi-modal sensing and fuse into a unified coordinate frame, storing as a structured spatial graph with feature attributes (geometry, classification, safety constraints). Embed worksite safety plan and operational policies as explicit constraint layers within the model. Expose via a constraint-aware query interface that returns features filtered by policy applicability and operational context, ensuring planning/execution functions receive policy-compliant feature sets.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between sensor fusion logic and spatial graph representation—mitigated by defining a clear sensor abstraction layer (DP does not prescribe sensor types).
Coupling between constraint representation and feature geometry—mitigated by layering constraints as orthogonal metadata rather than modifying feature primitives.
Coupling between model update frequency and planning cycle—mitigated by versioning and time-indexed queries, allowing asynchronous consumption.
Dependency on safety plan/policy specification format—risk if format is not standardized; recommend explicit schema definition as a separate FR.
coupling_check.notes: DP1+DP5 achieves functional independence: sensing/fusion is decoupled from constraint logic via metadata layering; query interface abstracts both from downstream consumers. Constraint embedding (DP5) does not force re-acquisition of spatial data, only re-interpretation. Modularity is preserved if sensor abstraction and constraint schema are clearly defined.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies are available in machine-readable form (or can be translated to it) before or concurrent with model generation. RISK: If policies are only documented in natural language or change frequently, constraint layer maintenance becomes a bottleneck. MITIGATION: Define a policy schema and version control mechanism.

ASSUMPTION: Multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) is feasible and sufficient to detect all required features. RISK: Occlusions, dynamic obstacles, or features not directly observable (e.g., underground utilities) may be missed. MITIGATION: Hybrid approach combining sensing with manual annotation or external data sources (e.g., site blueprints).

ASSUMPTION: A unified coordinate frame can be established and maintained with acceptable drift over the planning horizon. RISK: Localization error accumulation may degrade feature accuracy over time or large areas. MITIGATION: Periodic re-calibration, loop closure, or external reference points.

ASSUMPTION: Planning/execution functions can consume a constraint-aware query interface. RISK: If downstream functions expect a static, pre-filtered feature set, the query interface adds latency or complexity. MITIGATION: Provide both real-time query and periodic snapshot export modes.

ASSUMPTION: Feature classification taxonomy is agreed upon and stable. RISK: Misclassification or ambiguous features may cause planning failures. MITIGATION: Confidence scores and fallback handling in downstream functions.

ASSUMPTION: Spatial model update rate aligns with operational decision-making cycles. RISK: Stale features or missed transient obstacles. MITIGATION: Define acceptable staleness bounds and alert mechanisms.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly separate concerns: (1) FR-Spatial: Acquire and represent worksite geometry and feature locations in a machine-readable spatial model. (2) FR-Policy: Encode worksite safety plan and operational policies as constraint layers applicable to spatial features. (3) FR-Query: Provide a query interface that returns features and constraints filtered by operational context and planning horizon. This decomposition clarifies DP boundaries and reduces coupling. Additionally, add acceptance criteria specifying: (a) feature detection accuracy/recall targets, (b) constraint schema definition, (c) query latency and staleness bounds, (d) integration points with planning/execution functions (e.g., API contract, data format). Consider whether manual annotation or external data sources (blueprints, CAD models) should be incorporated as fallback or primary inputs, and whether the model must support dynamic feature updates (e.g., newly placed equipment) or is static for a given planning cycle.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized spatial data acquisition, multi-modal sensor fusion, unified coordinate frame management, spatial graph construction with feature attributes, safety constraint embedding, and constraint-aware query interface for policy-compliant feature delivery to planning and execution subsystems.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives raw multi-modal sensor data (LiDAR, camera, IMU, GNSS) from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems for spatial feature extraction and fusion.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits constraint violation warnings and policy applicability notifications when spatial features or operational context trigger safety constraint layers.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Delivers constraint-aware spatial feature sets (filtered by policy and operational context) to planning and execution functions requesting worksite geometry, classification, and safety-compliant operational boundaries.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Synchronizes spatial graph state, constraint layers, and policy updates across distributed nodes; receives remote sensor feeds and policy revisions.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment. NGHE Solution is the natural locus for integrated spatial intelligence: multi-modal sensor fusion, coordinate frame unification, spatial graph construction, and constraint-aware query logic all align with NGHE's core mission of unified environmental modeling. Safety constraint embedding and policy layers are metadata within the spatial model, not external enforcement. Minor diffusion: Vehicle/Teleoperation provide raw sensor streams (Telemetry in), and downstream planning/execution consume filtered features (Tasking out), but the DP logic resides entirely within NGHE.

Poor containment. Safety Services would naturally own constraint validation and policy enforcement, but the DP requires embedding constraints as explicit layers within the spatial graph model itself, not as external checks. Separating constraint storage from spatial geometry would force diffusion: Safety Services would need to query NGHE for features, then re-filter them, creating redundant logic and coupling. The DP's core insight—constraint-aware queries returning pre-filtered features—cannot be realized if constraints live outside the spatial model.

Very poor containment. Vehicle is a hardware/teleoperation platform, not a data fusion or spatial intelligence layer. Placing sensor fusion, coordinate frame management, and spatial graph construction in Vehicle would violate separation of concerns and create massive diffusion: planning, execution, and safety logic would all depend on Vehicle-resident spatial intelligence, creating tight coupling and poor modularity.

Very poor containment. Teleoperation is a command/control interface, not a spatial data fusion layer. Embedding spatial graph construction and constraint-aware queries in Teleoperation would conflate operator interaction logic with environmental modeling, creating high diffusion and poor reusability across autonomous and teleoperated modes.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The DP (integrated spatial feature acquisition, fusion, constraint embedding, and policy-aware query) is fundamentally a spatial intelligence and environmental modeling function. NGHE Solution is the designated module for unified environmental representation and is the only candidate with sufficient cohesion to contain the entire DP without major diffusion. Constraints and policies are metadata attributes of the spatial graph, not external enforcement mechanisms, so they belong within NGHE's data model. Downstream consumers (planning, execution, safety services) interact with NGHE via the constraint-aware query interface (Tasking and SafetyAlert), maintaining clean separation of concerns.
containment_risks: Risk: If Safety Services later requires real-time constraint validation independent of spatial queries (e.g., dynamic policy updates), the DP's assumption that constraints are embedded in the spatial graph may become insufficient. Mitigation: Design the constraint layer as a versioned, queryable sub-model within NGHE that Safety Services can subscribe to for updates.

Risk: Multi-modal sensor fusion is computationally intensive. If Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystems require low-latency local spatial awareness (e.g., collision avoidance), NGHE's centralized fusion may introduce unacceptable latency. Mitigation: Introduce a lightweight local spatial cache in Vehicle/Teleoperation that subscribes to NGHE's fused graph via Network Link, with NGHE remaining the authoritative source.

Risk: Constraint embedding within the spatial graph may create tight coupling between spatial geometry and policy logic. If policies change frequently or vary by operational mode, the graph may become difficult to maintain. Mitigation: Implement constraints as a separate, composable policy layer that is queried alongside geometry, not baked into feature attributes.

Risk: The constraint-aware query interface (Tasking) may become a bottleneck if many planning/execution functions request filtered feature sets. Mitigation: Cache common query results and implement efficient indexing on constraint attributes.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP choice is necessary. The DP is well-suited to NGHE Solution. However, refine the DP specification to clarify that constraint layers are queryable metadata within the spatial graph (not external enforcement), and that the constraint-aware query interface supports both synchronous (Tasking) and asynchronous (SafetyAlert subscription) modes to decouple policy evaluation from spatial geometry updates. This ensures NGHE remains the single source of truth for spatial intelligence while allowing Safety Services and planning/execution to consume constraints at their own cadence.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR ('Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model') is directly verifiable against acceptance criteria: (1) worksite model/map generation and availability to planning/execution functions, and (2) inclusion of agreed site features. The IM boundary (NGHE Solution) is the single authoritative module for spatial model construction and delivery via the Tasking interface. Verification can be performed by confirming that planning/execution functions receive constraint-aware feature sets matching the agreed feature taxonomy and that the spatial graph is queryable and policy-compliant.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Excellent. The FR is unambiguously allocated to NGHE Solution. The IM boundary clearly owns spatial data acquisition (Telemetry in), multi-modal sensor fusion, coordinate frame unification, spatial graph construction, constraint embedding, and constraint-aware query logic (Tasking out). External interfaces are well-defined: Telemetry (raw sensor input), Tasking (filtered feature delivery), SafetyAlert (constraint violation notifications), and Network Link (distributed synchronization). No ambiguity regarding ownership or responsibility.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The DP (integrated multi-modal sensing, fusion into unified coordinate frame, structured spatial graph with feature attributes and embedded safety constraints, constraint-aware query interface) directly and sufficiently addresses the FR. The DP specifies both the logical solution (how to acquire, fuse, and represent spatial features) and the delivery mechanism (constraint-aware queries ensuring policy compliance). The DP is traceable to acceptance criteria: the spatial graph is machine-readable, available to planning/execution via Tasking, and embeds worksite safety plan and operational policies as explicit constraint layers. No logical gaps or contradictions detected.

diffusion_risks: Constraint layer maintenance risk: If safety policies change frequently or vary by operational mode, embedding constraints as metadata within the spatial graph may create tight coupling and maintenance overhead. Mitigation: Implement constraints as a separate, composable, versioned policy sub-model queryable alongside geometry; avoid baking policies into feature attributes.

Latency risk for local spatial awareness: Multi-modal sensor fusion is computationally intensive and centralized in NGHE. If Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystems require low-latency local collision avoidance or reactive spatial awareness, NGHE's centralized fusion may introduce unacceptable latency. Mitigation: Introduce a lightweight local spatial cache in Vehicle/Teleoperation that subscribes to NGHE's fused graph via Network Link, with NGHE remaining the authoritative source.

Query interface bottleneck risk: The constraint-aware query interface (Tasking) may become a performance bottleneck if many planning/execution functions request filtered feature sets simultaneously. Mitigation: Implement efficient indexing on constraint attributes, cache common query results, and consider asynchronous subscription modes (SafetyAlert) for non-critical policy notifications.

Safety Services coupling risk: Safety Services has a containment score of 0.45 and diffusion score of 0.55. While the DP correctly places constraint storage within NGHE's spatial model, Safety Services may still require independent real-time constraint validation or dynamic policy updates. Mitigation: Design the constraint layer as a queryable, subscribable sub-model within NGHE that Safety Services can monitor for updates without re-filtering spatial features.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). NGHE Solution is the natural and only appropriate locus for the DP. All core logic—multi-modal sensor fusion, coordinate frame unification, spatial graph construction, constraint embedding, and constraint-aware query logic—resides within NGHE's boundary. Constraints and policies are metadata attributes of the spatial model, not external enforcement mechanisms, ensuring they remain co-located with geometry. Vehicle and Teleoperation are correctly positioned as data sources (Telemetry in) and consumers (Tasking out), not as spatial intelligence layers. Safety Services is correctly positioned as a consumer of constraint notifications (SafetyAlert) rather than as the owner of constraint storage. Minor diffusion (0.08 score) is unavoidable: Vehicle/Teleoperation provide raw sensor streams and planning/execution consume filtered features, but this is appropriate separation of concerns. The DP-to-IM mapping is clean, cohesive, and avoids redundant logic or scattered responsibilities.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP specification to explicitly state that constraint layers are queryable, versioned metadata within the spatial graph model, not external enforcement mechanisms. This clarifies the DP-IM boundary and prevents Safety Services from attempting to re-implement constraint validation outside NGHE.

Specify that the constraint-aware query interface (Tasking) supports both synchronous (request-response for planning/execution) and asynchronous (subscription-based SafetyAlert for policy notifications) modes. This decouples policy evaluation cadence from spatial geometry updates and reduces coupling between NGHE and downstream consumers.

Add a lightweight local spatial cache design pattern to Vehicle/Teleoperation subsystems that subscribe to NGHE's fused graph via Network Link. Document that NGHE remains the authoritative source and that local caches are read-only replicas for low-latency reactive operations (e.g., collision avoidance). This mitigates latency risks without violating the DP-IM allocation.

Implement constraint layers as a separate, composable policy sub-model within NGHE (not baked into feature attributes). Define a constraint query API that allows Safety Services and planning/execution to filter features by policy applicability without requiring NGHE to re-execute spatial queries. This reduces maintenance overhead and improves modularity.

Document the Network Link interface in detail: specify synchronization semantics (eventual consistency vs. strong consistency), conflict resolution for distributed policy updates, and subscription mechanisms for constraint layer changes. This ensures distributed nodes can reliably consume policy-compliant feature sets.

Add acceptance criteria verification plan: confirm that (1) worksite model is generated and available to planning/execution within agreed latency bounds, (2) all agreed site features are represented in the spatial graph, (3) constraint-aware queries return policy-compliant feature sets, and (4) Safety Services receives timely constraint violation notifications via SafetyAlert.

decision.justification: The FR-DP-IM gate results are all passing with high confidence (verification_feasibility: High, allocation_clarity: Excellent, containment: 0.92, diffusion: 0.08). However, the recommended_revisions and diffusion_risks reveal that the current DP specification, while logically sound, lacks sufficient detail to guide safe and efficient implementation. Specifically: (1) constraint layer architecture (queryable metadata vs. baked attributes) is underspecified; (2) latency mitigation via local spatial caching is not yet decomposed; (3) the Tasking interface's dual-mode operation (synchronous + asynchronous) requires sub-FR elaboration; (4) Network Link synchronization semantics are not yet detailed. Given high safety criticality, proceeding to next-level decomposition is justified to ensure these architectural decisions are explicitly captured as sub-FRs and traced through DP-IM allocation. This prevents implementation ambiguity and reduces post-design rework risk.
next_level_sub_frs: FR 2.1: Represent and version spatial constraints (safety policies, operational rules) as queryable, composable metadata within the spatial graph model, independent of geometry attributes.

FR 2.2: Provide constraint-aware query interface supporting both synchronous (request-response for planning/execution) and asynchronous (subscription-based SafetyAlert for policy notifications) modes.

FR 2.3: Maintain a lightweight, read-only local spatial cache in Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems that subscribes to NGHE's authoritative fused spatial graph via Network Link, enabling low-latency reactive spatial awareness without violating centralized authority.

FR 2.4: Synchronize distributed spatial models and constraint layers across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and NGHE via Network Link with defined consistency semantics (eventual vs. strong) and conflict resolution for policy updates.
FR 2.5: Detect and notify constraint violations (safety policy breaches) in real-time via SafetyAlert interface, enabling Safety Services to respond without re-filtering spatial features.

revise.why: The current DP specification correctly identifies the logical solution (multi-modal fusion, spatial graph, constraint embedding, constraint-aware queries) but does not explicitly detail: (1) whether constraints are queryable metadata or baked into feature attributes (critical for maintenance and Safety Services coupling); (2) the dual-mode operation of the Tasking interface (synchronous vs. asynchronous); (3) the architecture of local spatial caching in downstream subsystems; (4) Network Link synchronization semantics. These details are essential to prevent implementation divergence and to mitigate the identified diffusion risks (constraint maintenance, latency, query bottleneck, Safety Services coupling). Revise DP to explicitly specify: constraint layer as a separate, versioned, queryable sub-model; Tasking interface with both request-response and subscription modes; Network Link with defined consistency and conflict resolution; local cache pattern for Vehicle/Teleoperation. This revision will make the DP sufficiently detailed to guide safe, modular implementation.

assumptions: NGHE Solution is the single authoritative source for spatial models and constraint layers; no other subsystem owns or duplicates this logic.
Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems can tolerate eventual consistency with NGHE's spatial graph (i.e., local caches may lag by milliseconds to seconds).
Safety Services does not require independent, real-time constraint validation outside NGHE; it consumes constraint notifications via SafetyAlert and acts on them.
Network Link provides reliable, ordered message delivery with defined latency bounds (to be specified in sub-FR 2.4).
Constraint policies are versioned and can be updated dynamically without requiring spatial geometry re-computation.

Planning/execution functions can tolerate asynchronous policy notifications (SafetyAlert) for non-critical constraint updates; critical safety constraints are enforced synchronously via Tasking query responses.
open_risks: Constraint layer maintenance overhead: If safety policies change frequently or vary by operational mode, the queryable metadata approach may still introduce coupling between policy versioning and spatial graph versioning. Mitigation: Define a clear separation between geometry versioning and policy versioning; allow independent updates.

Local spatial cache coherence: If Vehicle/Teleoperation caches diverge from NGHE's authoritative graph due to network delays or failures, reactive collision avoidance may operate on stale data. Mitigation: Implement cache invalidation and refresh mechanisms; define acceptable staleness bounds; add fallback to conservative (safe) behavior if cache coherence cannot be guaranteed.

Tasking interface performance under high query load: If many planning/execution functions request constraint-aware feature sets simultaneously, the interface may become a bottleneck. Mitigation: Implement efficient indexing, caching, and consider asynchronous subscription modes; measure query latency in acceptance testing.

Network Link failure modes: If Network Link becomes unavailable, distributed nodes (Vehicle, Teleoperation) lose access to NGHE's authoritative spatial model. Mitigation: Define fallback behavior (e.g., use last-known-good cache, degrade to local sensing only); specify recovery mechanisms.

Safety Services real-time constraint validation: If Safety Services requires independent, real-time validation of constraints (not just notification consumption), the current DP may not satisfy its requirements. Mitigation: Clarify Safety Services' role in constraint validation; if independent validation is required, decompose as a separate FR and allocate to Safety Services with NGHE as a data source.
evidence_needed: Detailed DP specification for constraint layer architecture: define constraint metadata schema, versioning strategy, queryability API, and separation from geometry attributes.
Tasking interface specification: document both synchronous (request-response) and asynchronous (subscription) modes, including message formats, latency bounds, and error handling.
Network Link synchronization specification: define consistency model (eventual vs. strong), conflict resolution for policy updates, message ordering guarantees, and failure recovery.
Local spatial cache design pattern: specify cache invalidation triggers, staleness bounds, fallback behavior, and integration with Vehicle/Teleoperation subsystems.
SafetyAlert interface specification: define constraint violation notification schema, delivery latency, and integration with Safety Services.

Acceptance criteria verification plan: confirm that (1) constraint-aware queries return policy-compliant feature sets within latency bounds, (2) local caches remain coherent with authoritative graph within defined staleness bounds, (3) constraint violations are detected and notified in real-time, (4) Network Link synchronization handles policy updates without data loss or inconsistency.

Safety criticality analysis: confirm that the distributed spatial model architecture (with local caches and asynchronous notifications) does not introduce unacceptable safety risks; document safety case for fallback behavior and cache coherence mechanisms.
